header.title: InfraKit
header.subtitle: Self-service infrastructure provisioning platform with policy-as-code guardrails

summary.plain_english: A developer portal that lets engineers provision AWS infrastructure — VPCs, RDS instances, S3 buckets, and more — through a web UI without needing AWS console access. Every resource request is validated against a policy engine before deployment, ensuring cost caps, tagging standards, and security baselines are enforced automatically.

motivation.quote: I wanted to understand how platform engineering teams abstract cloud complexity away from developers while still enforcing governance. Building the guardrails layer — policy evaluation, drift detection, approval workflows — is where the real engineering challenge lives.

architecture.flow_summary: React UI → API Gateway → Lambda (policy eval) → Step Functions → CDK deploy → DynamoDB state store → SNS notifications

items: Policy-as-code engine — resource requests are evaluated against OPA (Open Policy Agent) rules before any infrastructure is provisioned, blocking non-compliant configs at the API boundary
AWS Step Functions orchestration — multi-stage approval and deploy workflows with human-in-the-loop steps for high-impact resources like RDS and VPC changes
Drift detection — scheduled Lambda compares live CloudFormation stack state against the desired state stored in DynamoDB, alerting on unauthorized changes
Cost estimation pre-deployment — integrates with AWS Pricing API to show estimated monthly cost before a request is approved
OIDC-based CI/CD — GitHub Actions pipeline deploys infrastructure updates using short-lived IAM role assumption, no static credentials stored
OPA integration with Lambda — embedding the OPA WASM binary inside a Lambda layer required careful bundle size management and cold-start profiling to keep policy evaluation under 200ms
Step Functions error handling — designing idempotent state machine steps that can be safely retried after a partial failure required explicit input/output schemas and careful use of ResultPath
Cross-account deployment — provisioning resources in target accounts required a hub-and-spoke IAM role assumption pattern with scoped trust policies per account
